skill.name: Apple HIG: Navigation Components
skill.description: Apple HIG guidance for navigation-related components including search fields, page controls, and path controls. Helps developers implement search UX, pagination, and breadcrumb navigation following Apple's design standards.
skill.summary: Search and navigation component guidance from Apple's Human Interface Guidelines
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 200 static analysis findings are false positives from pattern matching on Markdown documentation content. This skill contains only Apple Human Interface Guidelines reference documentation with no executable code. The detected 'external_commands' patterns are inline code formatting (backticks in Markdown), 'network' patterns are legitimate documentation links to developer.apple.com, and 'blocker' patterns match words in UI component names and API references. This is safe documentation content.

content.value_statement: Get Apple-approved search field, page control, and path control implementations that follow Human Interface Guidelines. Ensure your app's navigation feels native and intuitive across iOS, macOS, and other Apple platforms.
actual_capabilities: Provides Apple Human Interface Guidelines for search fields including scope controls, tokens, and instant results
Explains page control patterns for flat pagination with dot indicators and platform-specific behaviors
Documents path controls for macOS file hierarchy navigation and breadcrumbs
Covers platform differences for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, watchOS, and visionOS
Includes best practices for search placement, focus behavior, and result filtering
References official Apple API documentation for SwiftUI and UIKit implementations
limitations: Contains design guidance only - does not generate code implementations
Limited to Apple platforms - does not cover Android or web navigation patterns
Content is static reference documentation - not interactive or customizable
Requires developer to interpret guidelines and implement components themselves

use_cases.title: Implement Search in iOS App
use_cases.description: An iOS developer needs to add search functionality to their app. They use this skill to understand whether to place search in a tab bar, toolbar, or inline, and how to implement scope controls for filtering results.

use_cases.title: Add Pagination to Photo Gallery
use_cases.description: A developer building a photo gallery app needs page controls for navigation. They consult this skill to learn proper page control placement, indicator customization, and platform-specific behaviors for iOS vs watchOS.

use_cases.title: Design macOS File Browser
use_cases.description: A Mac developer is building a file browser and needs path controls for breadcrumb navigation. They reference this skill to understand standard vs popup path controls and proper placement in window body vs toolbar.
use_cases.target_user: macOS developer creating file management UI

prompt_templates.title: Basic Search Guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Where should I place the search field in my iOS app? The app has a tab bar at the bottom.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer starting search implementation needs placement advice

prompt_templates.title: Platform-Specific Page Controls
prompt_templates.prompt: How do page controls differ between iOS and watchOS? I'm building a fitness app that targets both platforms.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer needs to understand platform variations for pagination

prompt_templates.title: Scope Controls and Tokens
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm building a Mail-like app. How do I implement scope controls and tokens for search filtering?
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer implementing advanced search filtering features

prompt_templates.title: Path Control Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to show file hierarchy navigation in a macOS document app. Should I use standard or popup path controls, and where should I place them?
prompt_templates.scenario: Mac developer needs breadcrumb navigation design guidance

output_examples.input: Where should search go in an iOS app with a tab bar?
output_examples.output: For iOS apps with a tab bar, you have three main options: 1) Search as a visually distinct tab on the trailing side of the tab bar (keeps search always visible), 2) Search in a bottom toolbar (either expanded field or button that animates to field), or 3) Search inline with content. Apple recommends bottom placement when search is a priority and there's room, as it's easier to reach. Consider whether the search field should start focused (keyboard immediately appears) or unfocused (promotes discovery) based on your app's goals.

output_examples.input: What's the difference between page controls and path controls?
output_examples.output: Page controls display a row of indicator dots representing pages in a flat, linear sequence (like onboarding screens or photo galleries). They help users navigate between peers in an ordered list. Path controls show the file system path of a selected item, displaying the hierarchy from root disk to parent folders to the current item. Path controls are macOS-only and used for file navigation (like Finder's path bar), while page controls are for iOS/iPadOS pagination of discrete content pages.

output_examples.input: How do I add search scope tokens like in Mail?
output_examples.output: Tokens are visual representations of search terms that users can select and edit as filters. To implement tokens in your search field: 1) Define common search terms or items (like specific contacts in Mail or photo filters in Messages), 2) Apply visual treatment that encapsulates the term as a selectable item, 3) Let tokens filter additional search terms, 4) Consider pairing tokens with search suggestions so users discover available tokens, 5) Use SwiftUI's searchable APIs or UISearchTextField for token support. Tokens clarify searches by focusing them to specific attributes.

best_practices: Start search immediately when users type to provide instant, continuously refined results rather than waiting for form submission
Place search fields where users expect them - typically top of list, toolbar, or navigation bar - with helpful placeholder text describing what can be searched
Default to broader search scope and let users narrow it with scope controls, providing context for the full result set before refinement
anti_patterns: Avoid using generic placeholder text like 'Search' in search fields - it provides no helpful context about what content can be searched
Do not use page controls for hierarchical navigation - they are designed only for flat, linear page sequences where all pages are equally weighted
Never place path controls in toolbars or status bars - they belong in the window body, like Finder's path bar at the bottom of the window

faq.question: What platforms does this skill cover?
faq.answer: This skill covers all Apple platforms: iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, watchOS, and visionOS. It documents platform-specific considerations and behaviors for search fields, page controls, and path controls where applicable.

faq.question: Does this skill provide code examples?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides design guidance and best practices from Apple's Human Interface Guidelines. It references official Apple API documentation for SwiftUI and UIKit implementations but does not generate code itself.

faq.question: What's the difference between scope controls and tokens?
faq.answer: Scope controls are segmented controls that let users choose a search category before or during search (like All vs Current mailbox in Mail). Tokens are visual representations of specific search terms that act as filters (like filtering by a specific contact). You can use both together for powerful search filtering.

faq.question: When should I use focused vs unfocused search in a tab bar?
faq.answer: Use focused search (keyboard appears immediately) when you want quick, transient search experiences. Use unfocused search (field appears but keyboard waits) to promote discovery and exploration, especially in apps with large content collections like Music or TV.

faq.question: Can I use path controls on iOS?
faq.answer: No, path controls are macOS-only according to Apple's HIG. For iOS file hierarchy navigation, consider using navigation controllers, tab bars, or sidebars instead to represent hierarchical relationships.

faq.question: How many page indicators are too many?
faq.answer: Apple recommends avoiding more than about 10 page indicators because they become hard to count at a glance. If your app needs more than 10 pages as peers, consider using a different arrangement like a grid that lets people navigate content in any order.
